next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

By morning the floor had gone from packed earth to black paste. The men who had searched the house had left trunks open, baskets overturned, and the loft shelf half torn from the wall; the night rain had come through those new gaps as if it had been waiting outside for a way in. Mud sat in the heel dents of strangers. A brass plate leaned against a bed leg, furred with silt. One of Halima's clean birth cloths had slid into the doorway and drunk up the runoff until it looked like something used on a slaughter day. The house smelled of wet tin, mildew, old lamp smoke, and the sour paper smell that rose when ink started giving up.

Nura had already dragged the charpoy into the narrow stripe of gray light and was laying documents across the rope weave one by one. Her split lip had dried into a dark seam at the corner of her mouth. When she bent, Halima could see the small burn mark on her wrist flash pale against the wet brown of her skin, a little moon she had carried since she was thirteen and tried to lift the rice pot barehanded before it was off the stove.

"Not there," Halima said. "If the sun hits too hard, the paper will buckle."

Nura did not look up. "If the sun comes at all, we'll be lucky."

Her voice was flat from bad sleep and from holding pain in the mouth. She moved a marriage paper from the charpoy to an upturned tin trunk, then changed her mind and moved it back. The seal on it had already feathered at the edges. A yellowed land-tax receipt lay beside it, the numbers swelling loose from the page as if they wanted to float off and find a drier hous...
open_hooks_to_carry: Mofiz's interest in discrediting Halima is tied to his own threatened claims.
The search happens while Halima is structurally disadvantaged and cannot control the room.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...more than the decision itself, stripped the last ease out of Halima. Jamila would protect the life the lie had made, not because she mistook it for truth, not because she had forgotten the night of rescue, and not because duty had failed in her. She would protect it because a household built under false paper had become as bodily real as any child Halima had lifted wet and screaming into the world. To ask her to surrender it cleanly would be to ask her to pretend she had only ever been a girl being moved, never a woman who had since made meaning on the spot where she landed.

Halima stepped down into the yard. Majed, still inside, had taken up the rag again and was scrubbing grease from a wrench with a furious patience that would not fix anything but kept his hands from hanging empty. Jamila knelt over the papers, spine straight, binding the lie tighter because that was how a family survived the next week. Outside the fence, the older woman kept walking toward the western edge where the ground softened and the houses thinned.

Halima had once believed that if she cut a girl free quickly enough, the wound would close around the saving. In Jamila's house she saw what she had refused to count: cuts did not stay in the body that first received them. They traveled. They hardened into marriage lines, house posts, school names, resentments, debts, children, and old women with nowhere secure to stand when the boats came. She left the yard knowing that what she had called mercy all these years had grown roots of its own, and roots, once taken, were another way of making casualties.
state_deltas_required: Protect the ledger, the house, and Nura when rumor turns the island's fear against Halima.
The existence of a hidden ledger becomes public rumor, Nura is injured defending the house, and Halima's moral authority on the char is badly compromised.
